methodology.aggregation: Variance-weighted average ONLY when every study in the group reports an SE. When SE coverage is uneven, falls back to a simple mean across all studies with across-study sample variance for the CI — avoids over-weighting whichever paper happened to report SEs.
methodology.anti_indications: Treatments with studied AMF > 1 are kept and labeled — the studied direction is NOT a recommendation; see each entry's `direction` and `notes`.

treatments.name: Install cycle track / protected bike lane at intersection
treatments.description: Physically separated cycle track (raised, bollard-, or curb-protected) extending through the intersection.

studies.citation: Nosal, T. and L.F. Miranda-Moreno. "Cycle-tracks, bicycle lanes & on-street cycling in Montreal: a preliminary comparison of the cyclist injury risk." Presented at the 91st Annual Meeting of the Transportation Research Board, January 22-26, Washington, DC, 2012.

Nosal, T. and L.F. Miranda-Moreno. "Cycle-tracks, bicycle lanes & on-street cycling in Montreal: a preliminary comparison of the cyclist injury risk." Presented at the 91st Annual Meeting of the Transportation Research Board, January 22-26, Washington, DC, 2012.

treatments.notes: Clearinghouse evidence: 4 studies measuring 'install cycle tracks, bike lanes, or on-street cycling' at urban intersections.

treatments.name: Cycle track set back 2–5m from the main road with cyclist priority
treatments.description: Cycle track offset from the carriageway, with priority for cyclists at intersection crossings.

studies.citation: J.P. Schepers, J.P., Kroeze, P.A., Sweers, W., and Wust, J.C., "Road Factors and Bicycle-Motor Vehicle Crashes at Unsignalized Priority Intersections." Accident Analysis and Prevention, Vol. 43, Issue 3, Elsevier Ltd., (2011) pp. 853-861.

treatments.notes: European urban evidence (single study). Offset cycle track design that maintains cyclist priority at junctions, in contrast to standard pulled-back designs.

treatments.name: Raised bicycle crossing / vehicle speed-reducing measure at side road
treatments.description: Raised crossing surface or other speed-reducing geometry where vehicles enter/leave the side road.

studies.citation: J.P. Schepers, J.P., Kroeze, P.A., Sweers, W., and Wust, J.C., "Road Factors and Bicycle-Motor Vehicle Crashes at Unsignalized Priority Intersections." Accident Analysis and Prevention, Vol. 43, Issue 3, Elsevier Ltd., (2011) pp. 853-861.

treatments.notes: Single-study European evidence. Effect operates via vehicle speed reduction at the conflict point.

treatments.name: Install conventional (painted) bike lane
treatments.description: Standard on-street bike lane separated by paint only.

studies.citation: Avelar, R., B. Kutela, and X. Li. "Developing Crash Modification Factors for Bicycle Treatments at Intersections." Report No. FHWA-HRT-23-020. Federal Highway Administration. McVean, VA (2023).
Avelar, R., B. Kutela, and X. Li. "Developing Crash Modification Factors for Bicycle Treatments at Intersections." Report No. FHWA-HRT-23-020. Federal Highway Administration. McVean, VA (2023).

Avelar, R., B. Kutela, and X. Li. "Developing Crash Modification Factors for Bicycle Treatments at Intersections." Report No. FHWA-HRT-23-020. Federal Highway Administration. McVean, VA (2023).

treatments.notes: WARNING: studies show extreme heterogeneity (AMF range 0.16 to 1.43 across 4 studies). The averaged estimate masks substantial context-dependence — context where painted lanes help vs. hurt is not yet well characterised. Treat as low-confidence even though the mean is helpful.

treatments.name: Prohibit right-turn-on-red
treatments.description: Sign and enforce 'No turn on red' at the intersection.

treatments.notes: Clearinghouse cmid 114 measures the *permit* direction: permitting RTOR increases bike crashes ~77% across 8 studies. The recommended action is the inverse (prohibition), implied by inverting the AMF.

treatments.name: Convert yield/stop control to signalized
treatments.description: Install a full signal at a previously yield- or stop-controlled intersection.

treatments.notes: 10 urban studies. Mean effect on bike crashes is essentially zero (AMF ~1.04). Strong vehicle-KSI evidence does not transfer to bike-KSI here. List in the library so SDOT can see the bike-evidence honestly.

treatments.name: Install bike lane through a signalized intersection
treatments.description: Continue a painted bike lane through the conflict zone at a signalized intersection.

treatments.notes: 8 studies. Mean AMF ~1.08 — installing bike lanes at signalized intersections, on average, does not reduce observed bike crashes. Likely interpretation: facility raises bike exposure proportionally to risk reduction. This is why bike-volume (Strava Metro) is the critical missing covariate.

treatments.name: Convert intersection to single-lane roundabout (NOT recommended for bike-KSI)
treatments.description: Replace a conventional intersection with a single-lane modern roundabout.

treatments.notes: ANTI-INDICATION for bike-KSI. 25 studies, mean AMF ~1.59 — single-lane roundabouts INCREASE bike-intersection crashes ~59% relative to the conventional intersection they replace. This is the well-documented bike-roundabout paradox: roundabouts are safer for cars and pedestrians but more dangerous for cyclists due to entry/exit conflict geometry. The 'cmf' field below is the studied AMF as-measured; do NOT mis-read this as a recommendation. Surfaced in the library so SDOT planners considering a roundabout retrofit see the bike-specific evidence.

treatments.name: Modify signal phasing (implement a leading pedestrian interval)
treatments.description: Gives pedestrians a 3-7 second head start when entering the crosswalk.

studies.citation: Institute of Transportation Engineers, "Toolbox of Countermeasures and Their Potential Effectiveness to Make Intersections Safer." Briefing Sheet 8, ITE, FHWA, (2004)

treatments.notes: Leading Pedestrian Interval (LPI). Gives pedestrians a 3-7 second head start when entering the crosswalk. Proven to reduce pedestrian-vehicle crashes by increasing visibility and establishing pedestrian right-of-way.

treatments.name: Road diet (Convert 4-lane undivided road to 2-lanes plus turning lane)
treatments.description: Convert 4-lane undivided to 3-lane (2 through lanes + center turn lane).

studies.citation: Lim, L., and Fontaine, M. D. " Development of Road Diet Segment and Intersection Crash Modification Factors". Transportation Research Record 1–12, Transportation Research Board, Washington, D.C., (2022).

treatments.notes: Convert 4-lane undivided to 3-lane (2 through lanes + center turn lane). Reduces vehicle conflict points, lowers speeds, and provides space for pedestrian refuge islands or bike lanes.
